title: Inquiry, not performance
text: Socratic teaching is not a quiz show and not a courtroom ambush. It is a shared attempt to make a claim clearer than it was. The teacher’s ignorance is methodological: a willingness to test, not a refusal to help. If the learner needs a fact to stay safe, give the fact; then, if appropriate, return to questions.

title: Put a claim on the table
text: You cannot examine a vibe. Ask for one sentence the learner is willing to defend. If they offer three claims, pick one. If they offer none, offer a provisional claim and ask whether they accept it. The quality of the dialogue is bounded by the sharpness of the claim.

title: What do you mean?
text: Many arguments die in the dictionary they never wrote. When a key word does heavy work — justice, simple, secure, done, talent — ask for a definition in the learner’s own words. Then ask whether a borderline case still fits. Definitions are tools; if the tool slips, admit it and retune.

title: Example and counter-example
text: A claim that cannot survive an example is rhetoric. A claim that cannot survive a counter-example is dogma. Ask: give me a clear case where this is true. Now give me the most awkward case. If the awkward case breaks the claim, revise the claim; do not insult the case.

title: Elenchus without humiliation
text: Elenchus tests consistency: if A and B cannot both be true as stated, say so gently and invite repair. The target is the sentence, not the self-worth of the speaker. Humiliation produces silence or theater; neither is understanding. Prefer: ‘If we keep both claims, what follows that you would reject?’

title: Aporia is not failure
text: Aporia — a recognized stuckness — is a station, not a crash. Name what is unknown. Separate what is still solid from what collapsed. Choose the smallest next question that would unstick the work. False confidence is a more expensive failure than honest aporia.

title: Short synthesis, then a better question
text: After a productive stretch, offer a brief synthesis: here is the improved claim; here is what remains open. Then ask one next question. Long lectures break the method. If you must explain, explain briefly and return the ball.

title: When to stop questioning
text: Stop the method when the person is unsafe, exhausted, coerced, or asking for a concrete procedure they cannot discover by dialogue in time. Dignity outranks cleverness. Consent matters: a learner may ask for a direct answer; honor that, then optionally resume questions later.

title: Assume the opposite
text: A reliable test: assume the opposite of the learner’s claim and ask what breaks. If nothing breaks, the claim may be empty. If everything breaks, the claim may be load-bearing — then ask why. This is not contrarianism for sport; it is a stress test with a purpose.

title: Shared vocabulary
text: If two people use one word for two ideas, they will fight forever and call it principle. Pause and split the word: sense A and sense B. Ask which sense the claim needs. Many ‘deep disagreements’ dissolve into vocabulary debt.
